config.prompt: Name all 47 U.S. Presidents in order

items.explanation:
1st President (1789–1797), Federalist (no formal party)
Commander-in-Chief of the Continental Army during the Revolutionary War
Only president unanimously elected by the Electoral College — twice
Set the two-term precedent that held until FDR
Presided over the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia, 1787
Farewell Address warned against political parties and foreign entanglements
Died in 1799 at Mount Vernon; at death was the wealthiest president in history (adjusted)

2nd President (1797–1801), Federalist
First president to live in the White House (moved in November 1800)
Defended British soldiers in court after the Boston Massacre (1770)
Signed the Alien and Sedition Acts, which criminalized dissent
First president to be defeated for reelection
Died on July 4, 1826 — the same day as Thomas Jefferson, exactly 50 years after the Declaration
Father of John Quincy Adams (6th President) — first father-son presidential pair

3rd President (1801–1809), Democratic-Republican
Principal author of the Declaration of Independence (1776)
Engineered the Louisiana Purchase (1803), doubling U.S. territory for ~$15 million
Founded the University of Virginia; designed its campus himself
Sent Lewis and Clark on their expedition (1804–1806)
Inventor and polymath: redesigned the plow, built a revolving book stand, played violin
Died July 4, 1826 — same day as John Adams, on the Declaration's 50th anniversary

4th President (1809–1817), Democratic-Republican
'Father of the Constitution' — principal architect and author of the Bill of Rights
Shortest president at 5'4" and never weighed more than ~100 lbs
Led the nation through the War of 1812 against Britain
Only sitting president to personally command troops in battle (Bladensburg, 1814)
The White House was burned by the British during his presidency
His wife Dolley Madison famously saved Gilbert Stuart's portrait of Washington from the fire

5th President (1817–1825), Democratic-Republican
Issued the Monroe Doctrine (1823): the Americas are closed to further European colonization
Presidency known as the 'Era of Good Feelings' — ran virtually unopposed in 1820
Last president who was a Founding Father and Revolutionary War veteran
Wounded at the Battle of Trenton (1776) alongside Washington
Oversaw the Missouri Compromise (1820), temporarily resolving the slavery expansion debate
Died July 4, 1831 — the third president to die on Independence Day

6th President (1825–1829), Democratic-Republican / National Republican
Son of John Adams — first son of a president to become president (until George W. Bush)
Won the presidency via the House of Representatives ('Corrupt Bargain' of 1824)
Only former president to serve in the U.S. House of Representatives (1831–1848)
Fluent in seven languages; served as diplomat in Europe from age 14
Argued the Amistad case before the Supreme Court (1841), winning freedom for enslaved Africans
Collapsed on the House floor and died in the Capitol building, 1848

7th President (1829–1837), Democrat
Founder of the modern Democratic Party; his election called the 'rise of the common man'
Hero of the Battle of New Orleans (1815) — won decisively after the peace treaty was signed
First president to survive an assassination attempt (1835, both pistols misfired)
Destroyed the Second Bank of the United States, sparking the Panic of 1837
Forced the Indian Removal Act (1830), leading to the Trail of Tears
Nicknamed 'Old Hickory' for his toughness; fought in multiple duels, carrying bullets in his body

8th President (1837–1841), Democrat
First president born a U.S. citizen (not a British colonial subject)
First president born speaking a language other than English (Dutch, grew up in Kinderhook, NY)
Nicknamed 'The Little Magician' for his political skill and 'Old Kinderhook' (possibly the origin of 'OK')
Presidency dominated by the Panic of 1837, one of the worst financial crises in U.S. history
Lost reelection to William Henry Harrison in 1840 ('Tippecanoe and Tyler Too')
Ran again in 1848 on the Free Soil ticket, opposing the expansion of slavery

9th President (1841), Whig
Shortest presidency in history — died 31 days after inauguration
Delivered the longest inaugural address (nearly 2 hours, in cold rain without a coat)
Died of pneumonia (or possibly typhoid) on April 4, 1841
Campaigned with the famous slogan 'Tippecanoe and Tyler Too'
'Tippecanoe' referred to his 1811 victory over Tecumseh's confederacy at the Battle of Tippecanoe
Grandfather of Benjamin Harrison (23rd President) — only grandparent-grandchild presidential pair

10th President (1841–1845), Whig (expelled from party)
First VP to assume presidency upon a president's death — set the precedent for succession
Nicknamed 'His Accidency' by opponents who questioned his legitimacy
Expelled from his own party; his entire Cabinet resigned except Daniel Webster
Annexed Texas in 1845, one of the last acts of his presidency
Fathered 15 children — the most of any president
Died in 1862 as an elected Confederate congressman — the only president to join the Confederacy

11th President (1845–1849), Democrat
"Dark horse" candidate — first major surprise nominee in U.S. presidential history
Promised to serve only one term and kept it — one of the few presidents to voluntarily limit himself
Acquired Oregon Territory through negotiation with Britain and California/Southwest via the Mexican-American War
Oversaw the greatest territorial expansion since the Louisiana Purchase — added more land than any president except Jefferson
Died just 103 days after leaving office, the shortest retirement of any president
Considered the last strong president before the Civil War era
Signed the act establishing the Smithsonian Institution (1846)

12th President (1849–1850), Whig
Nicknamed "Old Rough and Ready" for his informal style and battlefield toughness
Hero of the Mexican-American War, especially the Battle of Buena Vista (1847)
Never voted in an election before being elected president himself
Died just 16 months into his term — possibly from gastroenteritis after eating cherries and iced milk at a July 4th celebration
Owned slaves but opposed the expansion of slavery into new territories
Second president to die in office (after William Henry Harrison)
His body was exhumed in 1991 to test for arsenic poisoning — results were negative

13th President (1850–1853), Whig
Last Whig president — the party collapsed shortly after his term
Assumed the presidency after Zachary Taylor's sudden death
Signed the Compromise of 1850, including the controversial Fugitive Slave Act
Often credited with installing the first bathtub and kitchen stove in the White House, though these claims are disputed
Later ran for president on the anti-immigrant Know-Nothing Party ticket in 1856
Helped found the University of Buffalo
Refused an honorary degree from Oxford, reportedly saying he had neither the literary nor scientific attainment to deserve it

14th President (1853–1857), Democrat
Only president from New Hampshire
Consistently ranked among the worst presidents by historians
Signed the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854), which led to the violent conflict known as "Bleeding Kansas"
His 11-year-old son Bennie was killed in a train accident weeks before the inauguration — Pierce and his wife witnessed the death
The only elected president not nominated for a second term by his own party
Arrested during his presidency for running over a woman with his horse (charges were dropped)
Close college friend of Nathaniel Hawthorne, who wrote his campaign biography

15th President (1857–1861), Democrat
Only president from Pennsylvania and the only president who never married (lifelong bachelor)
Served in Polk's Cabinet as Secretary of State and later as U.S. Ambassador to the United Kingdom
Presidency saw the Dred Scott Supreme Court decision (1857) and the secession crisis that led to the Civil War
Widely regarded as one of the worst presidents for failing to prevent the breakup of the Union
His niece Harriet Lane served as First Lady in the absence of a presidential spouse
Last president born in the 18th century (born 1791)

16th President (1861–1865), Republican
Preserved the Union through the Civil War and issued the Emancipation Proclamation (1863)
Delivered the Gettysburg Address, one of the most famous speeches in American history
First Republican president ever elected
Assassinated by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre on April 14, 1865 — died the next morning
Tallest president at 6'4" and largely self-educated as a frontier lawyer from Illinois
Established Thanksgiving as a permanent national holiday (1863)
Face on the $5 bill and the penny, and carved into Mount Rushmore

17th President (1865–1869), National Union/Democrat
First president to be impeached — acquitted by a single vote in the Senate
Assumed office after Lincoln's assassination; clashed bitterly with Radical Republicans over Reconstruction
Vetoed the Civil Rights Act of 1866, but Congress overrode the veto — the first major legislation passed over a presidential veto
Purchased Alaska from Russia for $7.2 million, a deal mocked as "Seward's Folly"
Never attended a single day of school — his wife taught him to read and write
Only former president to later serve in the U.S. Senate (elected 1875), until Andrew Johnson held that distinction alone among presidents

18th President (1869–1877), Republican
Commanded all Union forces in the Civil War and accepted Lee's surrender at Appomattox Court House (1865)
Real name was Hiram Ulysses Grant — "U.S." came from a clerical error at West Point that stuck
Presidency plagued by scandals including Credit Mobilier and the Whiskey Ring, though Grant himself was not personally corrupt
Established Yellowstone as the first national park (1872)
Wrote his memoirs while dying of throat cancer — published by Mark Twain, they became a critical and financial success
Face on the $50 bill
Served two full terms despite the scandals

19th President (1877–1881), Republican
Won the most disputed election in U.S. history (1876) — decided by a congressional commission after contested electoral votes
Nicknamed "Rutherfraud" by opponents who considered his presidency illegitimate
Ended Reconstruction by withdrawing federal troops from the South as part of the compromise that gave him the presidency
First president to have a telephone in the White House, installed by Alexander Graham Bell himself
Banned alcohol from the White House — his wife Frances was nicknamed "Lemonade Lucy"
Pledged to serve only one term and kept his promise

20th President (1881), Republican
Could simultaneously write in Latin with one hand and Greek with the other — he was ambidextrous
Assassinated by Charles Guiteau after only 200 days in office — shot on July 2, 1881 and died September 19, 1881
His death led directly to the Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act (1883), ending the spoils system
Was a Civil War general and the only sitting member of the House of Representatives ever elected president
Last president born in a log cabin
Last of four presidents to die in office up to that point (Harrison, Taylor, Lincoln, Garfield)

21st President (1881–1885), Republican
Nicknamed "The Gentleman Boss" for his elegant style and political connections
Assumed the presidency after James Garfield's assassination
Surprised everyone by championing civil service reform — signed the Pendleton Act despite being a product of the spoils system himself
Accused of being born in Canada rather than Vermont — an early "birther" controversy
Known for his lavish wardrobe and redecorating the White House with Tiffany glass and new furnishings
Had been a machine politician running the New York Customs House before the vice presidency
Suffered from Bright's disease (kidney failure) which he kept secret from the public

22nd and 24th President — the only president to serve non-consecutive terms
Only Democrat elected president between 1856 and 1912
Vetoed more bills than all previous presidents combined
Secretly had surgery for oral cancer on a friend's yacht in 1893 to avoid causing a market panic — the public didn't learn of it for decades
Known for personal honesty — "Tell the truth" was his guiding motto
Married Frances Folsom in the White House in 1886 — the only presidential wedding held there — making her the youngest First Lady at age 21
The Baby Ruth candy bar may have been named for his daughter Ruth, though this is disputed

23rd President (1889–1893), Republican
Grandson of William Henry Harrison (#9) — the only grandparent-grandchild pair in presidential history
Nicknamed "Little Ben" at just 5'6" tall
First president with electricity in the White House, but he and his wife were reportedly afraid to touch the switches
Signed the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890), the first federal law to limit monopolies
Admitted six states during his term: both Dakotas, Montana, Washington, Idaho, and Wyoming
Presided over the first "billion-dollar Congress" due to record federal spending
Lost the popular vote to Grover Cleveland in both 1888 (won Electoral College) and 1892 (lost both)

Same person as #22 — the only president to serve non-consecutive terms
Second term dominated by the Panic of 1893, the worst economic depression until the 1930s
Sent federal troops to break the Pullman Strike in 1894 without the request of the Illinois governor
Repealed the Sherman Silver Purchase Act to stabilize the gold standard
Lost the support of his own Democratic Party by the end of his second term
Left office deeply unpopular, with the country still mired in economic crisis

25th President (1897–1901), Republican
Last Civil War veteran to serve as president
Led the nation into the Spanish-American War (1898), gaining Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines
Presided over U.S. annexation of Hawaii (1898)
Assassinated by anarchist Leon Czolgosz at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York (September 1901)
His campaign pioneered the "front porch" strategy, delivering speeches from his home in Canton, Ohio
His face was on the $500 bill
His death elevated Theodore Roosevelt to the presidency

26th President (1901–1909), Republican
Youngest person to become president at age 42, after McKinley's assassination
Won the Nobel Peace Prize for mediating the Russo-Japanese War (1905) — first American Nobel laureate
Championed the national parks system and conservation, protecting roughly 230 million acres of public land
Built the Panama Canal, one of the greatest engineering feats of the era
"Trust Buster" who broke up monopolies under the Sherman Antitrust Act
The "Teddy Bear" was named after him following a hunting trip incident
Left the Republican Party to run as the Bull Moose (Progressive) candidate in 1912
Shot during a 1912 campaign speech in Milwaukee — finished the speech with the bullet still in his chest
One of four faces on Mount Rushmore

27th President (1909–1913), Republican
Only person in U.S. history to serve as both President and Chief Justice of the Supreme Court (appointed 1921)
Weighed over 300 pounds — the heaviest president in American history
Popular legend says he got stuck in the White House bathtub, though the story may be apocryphal
Handpicked by Theodore Roosevelt as his successor, but they became bitter political rivals — Roosevelt ran against him in 1912
His administration actually filed more antitrust suits than Roosevelt's, despite TR getting the "Trust Buster" label
Reportedly said the presidency was "the loneliest place in the world" — he much preferred the judiciary
First president to throw a ceremonial first pitch at a baseball game (1910)

28th President (1913–1921), Democrat
Led the nation through World War I after initially campaigning on keeping the U.S. out of the war
Proposed the League of Nations and won the Nobel Peace Prize (1919), but the U.S. Senate rejected American membership
Signed the Federal Reserve Act (1913), creating the modern central banking system
Signed the 19th Amendment (1920) granting women the right to vote
Suffered a severe stroke in October 1919 — his wife Edith effectively ran the executive branch for the remaining months of his presidency
Re-segregated the federal workforce, reversing progress made since Reconstruction
Only president with a PhD (political science, Johns Hopkins) and former president of Princeton University

29th President (1921–1923), Republican
Won in a landslide promising a "return to normalcy" after the upheaval of World War I
Presidency plagued by the Teapot Dome scandal — his Interior Secretary Albert Fall took bribes in exchange for oil drilling leases on federal land
Died suddenly in San Francisco in August 1923 — the cause was debated (likely a heart attack or stroke)
Appointed the first female director of the U.S. Mint (Nellie Tayloe Ross came later; he appointed the first female Bureau chief)
Coined the word "normalcy" as a political slogan, though the word already existed in English
Often ranked among the worst presidents by historians
His extramarital affair with Nan Britton, long rumored, was confirmed by DNA testing in 2015

30th President (1923–1929), Republican
Nicknamed "Silent Cal" — famous for being a man of few words
Sworn in by his own father, a notary public, by kerosene lamp at 2:47 AM after Harding's death in 1923
Presided over the Roaring Twenties economic boom and embraced laissez-faire economics
Famously declared "the chief business of the American people is business"
Vetoed a farm relief bill (McNary-Haugen) twice, angering agricultural states
Chose not to run for reelection in 1928, announcing simply "I do not choose to run for president in 1928"
His 16-year-old son Calvin Jr. died in 1924 from an infected blister he got playing tennis on the White House grounds — Coolidge said the presidency lost its appeal after the loss

31st President (1929–1933), Republican
Trained as a mining engineer — one of the few presidents with a technical background
Led massive humanitarian relief efforts feeding millions in Europe after World War I before entering politics
Presided over the 1929 stock market crash and the onset of the Great Depression
Shantytowns of the unemployed became known as "Hoovervilles" as a term of blame
Lived 31 years after leaving office — the longest post-presidency until Jimmy Carter surpassed him
Spoke Mandarin Chinese with his wife Lou to keep conversations private in the White House
Deeply unpopular when he left office, but later rehabilitated his reputation through public service

32nd President (1933–1945), Democrat
Only president elected four times — his unprecedented tenure led to the 22nd Amendment limiting presidents to two terms
Launched the New Deal, a sweeping set of programs to combat the Great Depression (Social Security, WPA, SEC, FDIC)
Led the United States through most of World War II, dying in office on April 12, 1945, just weeks before V-E Day
Paralyzed from the waist down by polio (diagnosed 1921) — used a wheelchair but was rarely photographed in it
Pioneered "fireside chats," radio addresses that made him a reassuring presence in American homes
Married to Eleanor Roosevelt, his fifth cousin once removed, who became the most activist First Lady in history
Face on the dime

33rd President (1945–1953), Democrat
Ordered the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki (August 1945), ending World War II
Desegregated the U.S. military by executive order in 1948
Led the U.S. into the Korean War (1950–1953)
Kept a sign on his desk reading "The Buck Stops Here"
Left office in 1953 with a 22% approval rating — the lowest of any president at that time — though historians now rank him highly
Never attended college — the last president without a college degree
Was a haberdasher (men's clothing store owner) before entering politics
Nicknamed "Give 'em Hell Harry" for his combative campaign style in the 1948 upset victory over Dewey

34th President (1953–1961), Republican
Supreme Allied Commander in World War II who planned and oversaw the D-Day invasion of Normandy (June 6, 1944)
Created the Interstate Highway System (1956), the largest public works project in American history
His farewell address warned of the dangers of the "military-industrial complex"
Sent federal troops to Little Rock, Arkansas to enforce school desegregation in 1957
Campaign slogan "I Like Ike" became one of the most iconic in presidential history
Both the Democratic and Republican parties tried to recruit him as their nominee before he chose the GOP
NASA was created during his presidency (1958) in response to the Soviet launch of Sputnik

35th President (1961–1963), Democrat
Youngest person ever elected president at age 43 (Theodore Roosevelt was younger when he assumed office, but was not elected to his first term)
First Catholic president
Navigated the Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962), the closest the world came to nuclear war
Created the Peace Corps by executive order in 1961
Assassinated in Dallas, Texas on November 22, 1963 — a date seared into American memory
"Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country" from his inaugural address
WWII hero who saved his crew after PT-109 was sunk by a Japanese destroyer
Won the Pulitzer Prize for "Profiles in Courage" (1957), though the extent of his authorship is debated

36th President (1963–1969), Democrat
Signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 — landmark legislation ending legal segregation
His "Great Society" programs created Medicare, Medicaid, and the War on Poverty
Escalation of the Vietnam War destroyed his presidency and divided the nation
Assumed office after JFK's assassination — sworn in aboard Air Force One with Jackie Kennedy standing beside him
Won the 1964 election by the largest popular vote margin in modern history
Known for a crude, dominating personality — the "Johnson Treatment" was his method of physically cornering people to get their votes
Chose not to run for reelection in 1968, announcing his withdrawal in a televised address

37th President (1969–1974), Republican
Only president to resign from office, leaving in disgrace over the Watergate scandal (August 9, 1974)
Opened diplomatic relations with the People's Republic of China in 1972 — a historic foreign policy shift
Created the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in 1970
Won reelection in 1972 carrying 49 out of 50 states — one of the largest landslides in history
Infamously declared "I am not a crook" during a press conference
Pardoned by Gerald Ford, sparing him from criminal prosecution
Served as Eisenhower's Vice President (1953–1961) and lost to JFK in 1960 in the first televised presidential debate

38th President (1974–1977), Republican
Only president never elected as either president or vice president — appointed VP after Spiro Agnew resigned, then became president when Nixon resigned
Pardoned Richard Nixon for all crimes related to Watergate — a decision that likely cost him the 1976 election
Survived two assassination attempts within 17 days in September 1975 (by Lynette "Squeaky" Fromme and Sara Jane Moore)
Was a star football player at the University of Michigan — turned down offers from the NFL's Detroit Lions and Green Bay Packers
Born Leslie Lynch King Jr. — his name was changed after his mother remarried Gerald Rudolff Ford Sr.
The only president from Michigan

39th President (1977–1981), Democrat
Brokered the Camp David Accords (1978), achieving a historic peace treaty between Israel and Egypt
Presidency overshadowed by the Iran hostage crisis (1979–1981), in which 52 Americans were held for 444 days
Won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2002 for decades of humanitarian work through the Carter Center
Was a peanut farmer from Plains, Georgia, and a trained nuclear submarine officer in the Navy
Installed solar panels on the White House roof — Ronald Reagan later had them removed
Longest-lived president in American history — died on December 29, 2024 at age 100
First president from the Deep South since Zachary Taylor (1849)

40th President (1981–1989), Republican
"The Great Communicator" — a former Hollywood actor and president of the Screen Actors Guild before entering politics
Oldest president at inauguration (69) until Donald Trump (70) and then Joe Biden (78) surpassed him
Survived an assassination attempt on March 30, 1981, quipping to surgeons "I hope you're all Republicans"
Challenged Gorbachev to "tear down this wall" at the Berlin Wall in 1987
Iran-Contra scandal: his administration secretly sold arms to Iran and funneled profits to Nicaraguan rebels
Enacted sweeping tax cuts and a massive military buildup during the Cold War
Won 49 states in his 1984 reelection, losing only Minnesota and D.C.
Diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease in 1994 after leaving office; was a Democrat until switching to Republican in 1962

41st President (1989–1993), Republican
Last World War II veteran to serve as president — was a Navy torpedo bomber pilot shot down over the Pacific
Former CIA Director (1976–1977) before becoming Reagan's Vice President
Famous broken promise: "Read my lips: no new taxes" — then raised taxes, which contributed to his 1992 reelection loss
Led the U.S. through the Gulf War (Operation Desert Storm, 1991), liberating Kuwait from Iraq
Father of George W. Bush (#43) — the second father-son presidential pair after John and John Quincy Adams
Parachuted from an airplane on his 90th birthday as part of a series of celebratory skydives
Overseen the end of the Cold War and the fall of the Berlin Wall during his presidency

42nd President (1993–2001), Democrat
Second president to be impeached (after Andrew Johnson) — acquitted by the Senate on charges related to the Monica Lewinsky scandal
Presided over the longest peacetime economic expansion in American history
Balanced the federal budget and achieved a surplus — the first since 1969
Signed NAFTA (North American Free Trade Agreement) into law
Campaign strategist James Carville's mantra "It's the economy, stupid" defined his 1992 victory
Rhodes Scholar who studied at Oxford University
Played saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show during the 1992 campaign — a defining moment of pop-culture politics
First Baby Boomer president (born 1946)

43rd President (2001–2009), Republican
Won the disputed 2000 election decided by Bush v. Gore — the Supreme Court halted a Florida recount, giving him the presidency by 537 votes
Led the post-9/11 response, launching wars in Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003)
Signed No Child Left Behind (2002) and the TARP bailout during the 2008 financial crisis
Former governor of Texas
Son of George H.W. Bush (#41) — only the second son of a president to become president (after John Quincy Adams)
Also the second president to win the Electoral College while losing the popular vote in the modern era (after Benjamin Harrison in 1888)
Before entering politics, co-owned the Texas Rangers baseball team

44th President (2009–2017), Democrat
First African American president in U.S. history
Signed the Affordable Care Act (2010), commonly known as "Obamacare," the most significant healthcare reform since Medicare
Ordered the military operation that killed Osama bin Laden in Abbottabad, Pakistan (May 2, 2011)
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2009, early in his first term
Former U.S. Senator from Illinois — served only one partial Senate term before winning the presidency
Campaign slogan "Yes We Can" became a cultural phenomenon
Born in Honolulu, Hawaii — the first president born outside the contiguous United States
Negotiated the Paris Climate Agreement (2015) and the Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA)

45th and 47th President — first president to serve non-consecutive terms since Grover Cleveland (#22/#24)
First president with no prior military or government service before taking office
First president impeached twice (2019 and 2021) — acquitted by the Senate both times
Won the 2016 election as a political outsider, defeating Hillary Clinton despite losing the popular vote
Built his career as a real estate developer and television personality (hosted "The Apprentice")
Presidency shaped by the COVID-19 pandemic beginning in 2020
Lost the 2020 election to Joe Biden; the January 6, 2021 Capitol breach followed the certification of electoral votes
Signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017), the largest tax overhaul since 1986

46th President (2021–2025), Democrat
Oldest person ever inaugurated as president at age 78
Served as Vice President under Barack Obama for two full terms (2009–2017)
First ran for president in 1988 — took over three decades to finally win the office
Served as a U.S. Senator from Delaware for 36 years (1973–2009), one of the longest Senate tenures in history
Oversaw the COVID-19 vaccine rollout and signed the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law
Withdrew from the 2024 presidential race, endorsing Vice President Kamala Harris
Lost his first wife Neilia and infant daughter Naomi in a 1972 car accident, and his son Beau died of brain cancer in 2015

Same person as #45 — second president to serve non-consecutive terms (after Grover Cleveland, #22 and #24)
Won the 2024 presidential election, defeating Kamala Harris
Oldest person to win a presidential election at age 78, surpassing Joe Biden's record
First president to return to office after being voted out since Grover Cleveland won in 1892
